foreshadow_text: “It is this,” I answered, producing a trowel from beneath the folds of my _roquelaire_.
payoff_text: As I said these words I busied myself among the pile of bones of which I have before spoken. Throwing them aside, I soon uncovered a quantity of building stone and mortar. With these materials and with the aid of my trowel, I began vigorously to wall up the entrance of the niche.
foreshadow_summary: Montresor produces a trowel from under his cloak as a sign of being a mason, which Fortunato dismisses as a jest.
payoff_summary: Montresor uses the same trowel to wall up the entrance of the niche, entombing Fortunato alive.

proposed_trigger.description: Montresor starts using the trowel for masonry
observable_conditions: P0074: 'With these materials and with the aid of my trowel, I began vigorously to wall up the entrance of the niche.'

relation_description: The trowel, initially presented as a joke, becomes the instrument of murder.
stage1_rationale: The trowel appears in a seemingly playful context and is later repurposed with deadly intent, spanning a significant narrative gap.

foreshadow_text: The thousand injuries of Fortunato I had borne as I best could; but when he ventured upon insult, I vowed revenge. You, who so well know the nature of my soul, will not suppose, however, that I gave utterance to a threat. _At length_ I would be avenged; this was a point definitively settled—but the very definitiveness with which it was resolved, precluded the idea of risk. I must not only punish, but punish with impunity. A wrong is unredressed when retribution overtakes its redresser. It is equally unredressed when the avenger fails to make himself felt as such to him who has done the wrong.
payoff_text:
“Yes,” I said, “let us be gone.”

“_For the love of God, Montressor!_”

“Yes,” I said, “for the love of God!”
foreshadow_summary: The narrator declares that revenge must be accomplished without risk to himself and the victim must know who is avenging him.
payoff_summary: Fortunato, realizing his fate, cries out 'For the love of God, Montresor!' acknowledging Montresor as his murderer, while Montresor goes unpunished.

observable_conditions: P0082: 'For the love of God, Montresor!'

relation_description: The specific rules of revenge set at the very beginning are fulfilled when the victim identifies his killer at the climax.
stage1_rationale: The opening philosophical statement directly maps to the climactic recognition, demonstrating delayed fulfillment across the entire narrative.

foreshadow_text:
I said to him: “My dear Fortunato, you are luckily met. How remarkably well you are looking to-day! But I have received a pipe of what passes for Amontillado, and I have my doubts.”

“How?” said he. “Amontillado? A pipe? Impossible! And in the middle of the carnival!”

“I have my doubts,” I replied; “and I was silly enough to pay the full Amontillado price without consulting you in the matter. You were not to be found, and I was fearful of losing a bargain.”

“Amontillado!”

“I have my doubts.”

“Amontillado!”

“And I must satisfy them.”

“Amontillado!”

“As you are engaged, I am on my way to Luchesi. If any one has a critical turn, it is he. He will tell me—”
payoff_text:
“The Amontillado!” ejaculated my friend, not yet recovered from his astonishment.

“True,” I replied; “the Amontillado.”
foreshadow_summary: Montresor lures Fortunato with the promise of a pipe of Amontillado, exploiting his wine expertise.
payoff_summary: After chaining Fortunato, Montresor ironically echoes 'The Amontillado' and begins walling him in, revealing the lie; there is no wine.

proposed_trigger.description: Montresor stops pretending about the Amontillado and starts masonry
observable_conditions: P0074: 'As I said these words I busied myself among the pile of bones... I soon uncovered a quantity of building stone and mortar.'

relation_description: The false promise of wine leads to a fatal trap, and the payoff is the unmasking of the lure.
stage1_rationale: The central deception is set up early and paid off when the truth is revealed through action rather than explanation, creating dramatic irony.
